text.title: SBI Model, SBII Extension, and Radical Candor
text.summary: The Situation-Behavior-Impact model for structuring feedback, its Intent extension for opening dialogue, and the Radical Candor quadrant for calibrating delivery tone.

rule.statement: Deliver feedback using the SBI structure: Situation (specific context), Behavior (observable action), Impact (concrete effect on team, project, or person).
rule.rationale: SBI decouples observation from judgment. The recipient can act on "you interrupted the client twice" (behavior); they cannot act on "you were rude" (character). Specificity eliminates the "when?" ambiguity that makes feedback forgettable.

example.code: Situation: "In yesterday's client meeting..." Behavior: "When you interrupted the client twice..." Impact: "The client seemed frustrated and we lost momentum."
"You're impatient and you don't listen to clients."

rule.statement: Add an Intent inquiry ("Help me understand what you were trying to do?") after the Impact step when the behavior could have a legitimate reason you don't know.
rule.rationale: SBII prevents the fundamental attribution error — assuming the person acted from a negative motive when there may be a good reason. It converts feedback delivery into dialogue.

example.code: "In the code review (S), when you approved without comments (B), I was surprised because that PR had several issues (I). Can you help me understand your thinking? (Intent)"

rule.statement: Deliver feedback that is simultaneously caring (you want the person to succeed) and direct (you challenge the behavior clearly). Avoid both ruinous empathy and obnoxious aggression.
rule.rationale: Kim Scott's research shows most feedback failures are in the "ruinous empathy" quadrant — feedback so softened it communicates nothing actionable. The recipient feels good but does not change.

list.item: Radical Candor (goal): "I care about your growth, and this needs to change"
Ruinous Empathy (avoid): "It's fine..." when it is not
Obnoxious Aggression (avoid): "This is garbage, what were you thinking?"
Manipulative Insincerity (avoid): talking behind back, passive-aggressive

rule.statement: Apply Radical Candor in sequence: ask first ("Can I give you some feedback?"), be specific with SBI, be immediate (within 48 hours), be private.
rule.rationale: Asking permission engages the recipient as a participant rather than a subject, reducing defensiveness before the content is delivered.
